title: Solvable Random Unitary Dynamics in a Disordered Tomonaga-Luttinger Liquid
abstract: Disordered one-dimensional interacting systems have long been characterized via conventional correlation functions. A complementary quantum-information perspective quantifies the randomness of the unitary ensemble dynamics generated by a quantum system through the frame potential, which serves as a practical diagnostic for quantum algorithmic performance. However, no analytical treatment has yet been achieved for experimentally accessible interacting one-dimensional systems. In this Letter, we derive a closed-form expression for the frame potential of a Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid with quenched Gaussian forward-scattering disorder. Exploiting the exactly quadratic structure of the disorder-averaged Keldysh action, we show that the frame potential decays as a power law at early times and saturates to a late-time plateau controlled by a single coupling parameter. Taking the random field XXZ spin chain as a specific microscopic realization, we show that the strongest randomness is achieved near the Heisenberg ferromagnetic point and can be exponentially enhanced through a multiple-quench protocol. We validate our results across the entire gapless phase, with direct implications for algorithm design in analog quantum simulation platforms.
summary: This paper provides the first analytical description of how unitary dynamics in a disordered 1D interacting system approach Haar randomness. By studying the Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid, the authors show that the frame potential reaches a plateau determined by a single coupling parameter. They also demonstrate that randomness can be enhanced using multiple-quench protocols and that approaching the ferromagnetic limit maximizes scrambling. These findings offer practical guidance for designing analog quantum simulators.
main_problem: The lack of an analytical treatment for the randomness of unitary ensemble dynamics, specifically the decay of the frame potential, in interacting 1D systems with disorder.
main_result: The authors derive a closed-form expression for the frame potential of a Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid with Gaussian forward-scattering disorder, showing it decays as a power law at early times and reaches a late-time plateau.
method: The study employs bosonization and the Schwinger-Keldysh formalism, utilizing the matrix determinant lemma to evaluate the disorder-averaged Keldysh action.
model_or_system: A Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid (TLL) with quenched Gaussian forward-scattering disorder, with a microscopic realization provided by a random-field XXZ spin chain.
key_observables: Frame potential (FP) and the normalized frame potential ratio R(k).
important_parameters: Luttinger parameter (K), sound velocity (u), disorder strength (gamma), dimensionless coupling (g), and anisotropy (Delta).
main_assumptions: The disorder is restricted to the forward-scattering regime, and the analysis focuses on the gapless phase of the TLL at low temperatures.
figures_summary: Figure 6 compares different filter orders (p=1, 2, 3) to show how higher-order filtering improves agreement between theory and numerics; Figure 7 shows the time evolution of the FP ratio for various anisotropy and disorder strengths.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of quantifying unitary randomness, presents the analytical derivation using Keldysh field theory, validates the results using TEBD and exact diagonalization, and discusses optimization via multiple quenches and filtering.
why_interesting: It provides a direct analytical link between many-body parameters (like compressibility and interaction strength) and the performance of quantum algorithms, specifically regarding how effectively a system can act as a quantum design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Free-fermion benchmark at ∆= 0 with σh = 0.05. In each panel, curves for k = 1, . . . , 5 are shown from top to bottom. (a) L = 64. Solid lines are free-fermion numer- ics from exact diagonalization. Dashed lines denote TEBD benchmark data, available up to T = 64. Dotted lines are the field-theory prediction using the discrete-momentum formula in Eq. (9). (b) L = 24 with all other settings unchanged. In both cases, a single UV cutoff α is obtained from a k = 1 and then kept fixed for all k.

FIG. 2. Time dependence of the normalized FP R(k)(T) for the interacting XXZ chain with L = 64, J = 1 and σh = 0.05 at ∆= −0.7, −0.5, 0.0, 0.5 with panels (a)–(d). For each panel from top to bottom, the different colors label k = 1 . . . 5. Solid curves with shaded uncertainty are TEBD numerics. Dotted curves are the TLL prediction from Eq. (9). For each ∆, a single UV cutoff α is obtained from a k = 1 fit in an intermediate-time window and then kept fixed for all k.

FIG. 3. Late-time one-parameter collapse. From the late- time plateau with last 10% of time points near T ∼64, we plot α ln R(k)(∞) versus αgk with g = 8πKγ/u2 and γ = σ2 h/π2, for k = 1, . . . , 5, σh = 0.05, 0.2, and selected anisotropic pa- rameters ∆= −0.7, 0.0, 0.5. The black curve is the analytic prediction from Eq. (10), with no additional fitting.
FIG. 4. Multiple-quench FP decay with ∆= 0, L = 50, σh = 0.05, and p = 1. (a) Normalized FP ratio R(k) m ({Tj}) versus total evolution time T = P
FIG. 5. The illustration of the Keldysh contour for the FP with k = 1. The solid lines represent the contours and the dashed lines represent the disorder coupling.

FIG. 6. Comparison of filter orders p = 1, 2, 3 for the XXZ chain with L = 64, ∆= 0.5, and σh = 0.05. Solid curves are TEBD data and dotted curves are the TLL prediction from Eq. (9), with k = 1, . . . , 5 from top to bottom in each panel. The UV cutoff α is fitted from k = 1 and held fixed for all k. Increasing p sharpens the suppression of the 2kF disorder component and improves the theory and numerics agreement.

FIG. 7. Time dependence of the normalized FP Rk(T) for the interacting XXZ chain with L = 64, J = 1, ∆= −0.7, −0.5, 0.0, 0.5, and σh = 0.05 for panels (a)–(d) and σh = 0.2 for panels (e)–(h). In each panel, different colors cor- respond to k = 1, . . . , 5 from top to bottom. Solid curves with shaded uncertainty bands are TEBD numerics. Dashed curves are the TLL prediction from Eq. (9). For each ∆, a single UV cutoff α is extracted from a k = 1 fit in an intermediate time window and held fixed for all k.
